payload.text:
Build a complete full-stack web app called myCORE for Growth Nexis Global, a routine-centered habit system with an Apple-style clean aesthetic. The daily routine is the CORE, and users don’t manually tick tasks — all habits are auto-triggered whenever possible.

UI Requirements:

Color palette: White, Grey, Navy Blue

Soft shadows, 16–24px rounded cards

Thin, elegant lines, minimalistic icons instead of emojis

Subtle animations, smooth transitions

Header includes Growth Nexis Global logo

Footer includes tagline: “…Unlocking Limitless Potential. Delivering Global Impact.”

Mobile-first, responsive layout

Top tabs: Health, Productivity, Finance, Learning, Detox

Weekly dashboard (Mon–Sun) with circular progress rings, completion %, habit tracker grid, and analytics bar + ring charts

Habit System:

Habit properties: name, icon, interest (category), schedule (daily, weekdays, custom, or time-based), strictness (auto-complete or confirm)

Daily habit instances generated automatically

Include 3 auto-trigger simulations:

Location Trigger – mark habit complete when entering a geofenced location (simulated endpoint)

App-Open Trigger – mark habit complete when interacting with a reading or activity page

Screen-Time Trigger – mark habit complete if daily screen time is below threshold

Auto-triggers run on serverless endpoints that update habit_instances table

Onboarding Flow:

Welcome screen with Growth Nexis Global logo

Select interests (Health, Productivity, Finance, Learning, Detox)

App suggests 5 habits automatically, with minimal icons

Prompt user to enable location, notifications, and screen time input

Generate daily habit instances for first week

Home Dashboard (Weekly View):

7-day card layout (Mon–Sun) with circular progress rings, completion %, and habit list

Habit tracker grid on the right (rows = habits, columns = days)

Top analytics: weekly streaks, progress rings, bar charts

Optional confirmation for habits requiring manual validation

Interests Tabs:

Each tab shows suggested habits, articles, videos, micro-tasks

Displayed in rounded, minimalistic cards

Progress auto-tracked via triggers

Analytics Page:

Weekly streaks, habit strength score, total completion rings

Progress per interest

Apple-style bar + ring charts

Settings:

Edit habits, export CSV, privacy notice, delete account

Tech Stack & Backend:

React + Tailwind CSS

Firestore (or equivalent) with tables: users, interests, habits, habit_instances

Serverless functions handle triggers and update habit instances

Seed demo user + sample habits

Functional weekly dashboard + habit tracker grid

Deliverables:

Self-contained React file (myCORE.jsx) with Tailwind styling, database integration, simulated auto-trigger engine, and demo data

README.md explaining architecture, database schema, and instructions to extend auto-triggers

Generate everything as a single project bundle ready for deployment. Include:

All UI components, state management, serverless trigger simulations

Build a full-stack web app called myCORE.  
The app is a routine-centered habit system with an Apple-style clean aesthetic.  
The daily routine is the CORE of the app.  
Users don’t manually “tick” tasks — tasks are triggered or auto-detected whenever possible.

-----------------------------------
🌟 CORE FEATURES
-----------------------------------

1. Home Dashboard (Weekly View)
- Weekly layout (Mon–Sun) shown as 7 cards.
- Each card has:
  • a circular progress ring  
  • list of tasks/habits for that day  
  • completion %  
- A Habit Tracker grid on the right (rows = habits, columns = days).
- At the top: weekly analytics (bar chart + ring).

2. Interests Tabs
Tabs across the top:
- Health  
- Productivity  
- Finance  
- Learning  
- Detox  

Each tab contains:
- Suggested habits (auto-loaded)  
- Articles / videos / micro-tasks the user must consume every 3 days  
- Clean Apple-style UI with neutral palette, rounded cards, soft shadows.

3. Habit System (NOT manual ticking)
A Habit has:
- name  
- emoji  
- interest (category)  
- schedule (daily, weekdays, custom days, or time-based)  
- auto triggers (see below)  
- strictness (auto-complete or ask user to confirm)  
Habits generate daily “habit instances” automatically.

4. Automatic Trigger Engine
Implement basic version of 3 auto-triggers:

A) Location Trigger (Gym example)  
- When the user enters a geofenced location for X minutes  
- Auto-complete the habit for that day  
- Use simple simulated location endpoint

B) App-Open Trigger (Reading example)  
- When the user opens a selected app or reading source  
- Simple version: detect when user interacts with a “Start Reading” button or reading page  
- Mark habit as complete

C) Screen-Time Trigger (Social Media Detox)  
- Allow user to input screen time for the day  
- If below threshold → mark habit complete  
- Simple input field for MVP

Triggers should run on a small serverless function that marks habit_instances.

5. Onboarding Flow
- Select interests  
- App suggests 5 habits automatically  
- Ask user to enable:  
  • location  
  • notifications  
  • screen time input  

6. Analytics Page
- Weekly streaks  
- Habit strength score  
- Total completion rings  
- Progress of each interest  
- Clean Apple-style charts

7. Settings
- Edit habits  
- Export data (CSV)  
- Privacy notice  
- Delete account  

-----------------------------------
🌟 UI STYLE
-----------------------------------
- Apple aesthetic  
- White / off-white background  
- Soft shadows  
- Rounded 16–24px cards  
- Thin, elegant lines  
- Minimal icons  
- Subtle animations  
- Smooth transitions  
- Consistent spacing, airy layout  
- Premium feel, not cluttered

-----------------------------------
🌟 TECH REQUIREMENTS
-----------------------------------
- Full-stack generated in Bolt  
- Database with tables:
  users  
  interests  
  habits  
  habit_instances  
- Serverless endpoints for triggers  
- React + Tailwind UI  
- Mobile-first responsive design  
- Seed demo user + sample habits  
- Working weekly dashboard + tracker grid  

-----------------------------------
🌟 FINAL DELIVERABLE
-----------------------------------
Generate the complete working MVP:
- full UI  
- backend logic  
- database schema  
- sample data  
- functional trigger simulation  
- and a README explaining how to extend the auto-trigger engine  

Project name: myCORE – MVP Build
Clean, minimalistic Apple-style UI

Growth Nexis Global logo and tagline throughout

Colors: White, Grey, Navy Blue

Minimal icons instead of emojis

Mobile-first responsiveness

Functional auto-trigger system for habits

**Refining Core Concept**

I'm zeroing in on deconstructing the request. "myCORE" is the app name, and the core concept revolves around a routine-centric habit system. The aim is to create a "Core" daily routine with auto-triggered habits to minimize manual tracking. This is foundational to the suggested codebase.


**Envisioning App Design**

I'm now deeply visualizing the app's look and feel, inspired by Apple's clean aesthetic. I've broken down the structural components, from header to footer, including the necessary views: Onboarding, Home, Interests, Analytics, and Settings. The functional requirements are coming into focus, with the Habit System as the core, including triggers based on location or app interaction. The technical stack involves React, TypeScript, Tailwind, and a simulated backend.


**Analyzing Folder Structure**

I've been meticulously examining the folder structure and fleshing out the components.  The `index.html` file utilizes Tailwind CDN, and the `index.tsx` file serves as the entry point. Key types are defined in `types.ts`, while `constants.ts` stores defaults and categories.  I'm concentrating on the logic within `triggerEngine.ts` to make sure it handles auto-triggers correctly. The UI components are also solidifying with the use of Lucide-React for icons.


**Planning Visual Elements**

I'm now contemplating design details. Should I lean towards `rounded-2xl` or `rounded-3xl`? Decisions like these are critical for the app's visual style.

\n\n

I've finally solidified the implementation plan. I'm focusing on setting up the data model: Habits with IDs, triggers, and schedules, as well as HabitInstances linked to dates and statuses. Step two is to design a mock database service. After that, I will implement a trigger engine for location, app open, and screen time habits. Next, it's all about layout components, the onboarding flow, and the "CORE" dashboard with its weekly summary, daily cards, and habit grid. Finally, I will build out the analytics section.


**Optimizing UI Details**

I'm now fully immersed in the design phase. I've chosen `recharts` for the visualizations. I'm focusing on color, spacing, and animations, leaning toward simple CSS transitions within Tailwind. My global context will house user info, habits, and today's instances, representing a database subscription. Date generation and icon usage, specifically `lucide-react` with navy blue for active states, are also being addressed. I'm avoiding pure black, utilizing dark navy, and designing white cards on light grey, all with crisp headings and minimal charts. A "simulation" panel is being drafted for auto-trigger visualization.


**Designing Task Interaction**

I'm now refining the daily task actions based on trigger types. Manual tasks will have checkboxes, location-based ones will offer a "Simulate Arrival" option, screen time tasks will need time input, and app open tasks require a "Read" prompt.

\n\n
I'm assembling the codebase, leveraging React, ReactDOM, Lucide-React, Recharts, clsx, and tailwind-merge. I've added Inter font to `index.html` and defined the `TriggerType` enum in `types.ts`. I'm populating seed data in `services/storage.ts` to simulate the backend. Simultaneously, I'm building UI components. To address the requirement for serverless functions, I've designed a `TriggerService` that returns delayed Promises, emulating network calls to a backend. Finally, I'm integrating blur effects with `backdrop-blur-md` for the "Apple aesthetic".


**Concluding App Construction**

I'm now putting the finishing touches on the app. I've integrated `bg-white/80` for that extra visual polish and am meticulously reviewing all components. I'm confirming that there are no CSS files, that Typescript is correctly applied, and that all Tailwind and Lucide-React elements are rendering as expected. Finally, I'm verifying responsiveness across the design. The circular progress rings are also getting a once-over.


**Refining Mobile Responsiveness**

I'm now focusing on mobile optimization. I'm ensuring the app's layout is not too wide for smaller screens, and I'm deciding between a horizontal scroll or a more condensed view for mobile.

Here is the complete full-stack React web application for **myCORE**.

It includes a simulated backend, a robust habit engine with auto-triggers, an Apple-inspired UI system using Tailwind CSS, and a complete onboarding-to-dashboard flow.

payload.text: Integrate a notification system into the myCORE app. Send reminders to users for habits that require manual confirmation. Also, implement encouraging notifications for streaks or achieving daily goals. Ensure notifications are opt-in and configurable by the user.

**Defining Notification Types**

I've been mapping out the specific notification types needed: reminders for manual habits, encouraging messages for streaks, and daily goal celebrations. The architecture is becoming clearer, and I'm looking at potential integration points within the existing myCORE app structure to determine where to place the notification service or manager. The goal is a seamless, user-configurable experience.


**Designing the Notification System**

I'm now focusing on integrating the notification system. Since this is a browser-based web app, I'm leaning toward using the native `Notification` API with fallbacks to in-app alerts if permissions are denied. The core concept is a `NotificationService` that handles permission requests and notification delivery. I'm expanding the `User` settings type to encompass more granular notification preferences, like specific reminder times, and I'll soon be adding logic to check for due reminders within the `App.tsx` file to maintain functionality.


**Finalizing Integration Points**

I'm deep into integrating notifications now. I'm focusing on updating `App.tsx` to initialize the notification service and regularly check for incomplete manual habits, perhaps using `useEffect`. The SettingsPage is next, where I'll add a notification toggle and maybe a test button. I'm also ensuring settings are properly saved in `mockDb.ts`. Users must log in or sign up before accessing the dashboard.

Use Firebase Auth (email/password or Google login).

name

icon

schedule

location-based triggers

activity tracking

screen time input

Generate the user’s first weekly routine using:

the 5 suggested habits

plus any custom habits added by the user

**Integrating Authentication**

I'm currently focused on integrating Firebase Auth into `myCORE`. Right now, it leans on a mock database and `localStorage`. My immediate task involves configuring Firebase, although a real project isn't feasible right now. The goal is a secure authentication system, even if initial implementation relies on some mock components. I anticipate needing to refactor the data storage strategy to align with Firebase's authentication flow.


**Reflecting on Firebase Integration**

I've been wrestling with how best to integrate Firebase Auth given the simulated environment. Initially, I considered mocking Firebase Auth or providing a credential structure. However, the explicit instruction to "use Firebase Auth" suggests client-side SDK usage is necessary, even if a backend connection is unconfirmed. The goal is to simulate a functioning frontend that *uses* Firebase, aligning with the "simulated auto-trigger engine" aspect. I'll need to focus on structure rather than full backend integration.


**Developing Auth Simulation**

I'm working on an `AuthService` to simulate Firebase Auth, given the explicit instruction to use it. I'll include the Firebase SDK imports and code structure. If API keys are unavailable, the service will default to a mock implementation, keeping the UI flow intact. This approach allows for UI/UX testing as if real Firebase Auth is present. The `mockDb.ts` file may need modification or replacement. The goal is a seamless authentication experience, even without backend keys, while retaining a Firebase-ready architecture.


**Rethinking Authentication Simulation**

I'm reconsidering the auth simulation approach. While aiming for a Firebase-like experience is helpful, I'm leaning toward a more robust mock. A broken Firebase integration would be less ideal in the preview environment. The core focus is maintaining the authentication flow with the new user onboarding: login/signup, interest selection, habit suggestions (including custom habits), permission granting, and week generation. The `App.tsx` component requires separating the `user` (authentication) and `userProfile` (data). I'll have a generic "Loading" state initially, then redirect based on auth status and onboarding completion, updating `mockDb.ts` to reflect this async auth process.


**Defining Authentication Workflow**

I'm now outlining a clear authentication flow with new methods in `mockDb.ts`: `signIn` and `signUp`, rather than dedicated auth user management. The goal is straightforward: `getUser()` fetches the profile, while `signIn` and `signUp` control the auth state. I'm focusing on simplicity, adding login/signup forms in `components/Auth.tsx` and integrating custom habit creation within `Onboarding.tsx`, alongside a revised `App.tsx` that manages the auth and onboarding process. I am carefully evaluating all of these changes so I can make them work seamlessly.


**Simulating Authentication Flow**

My plan is to integrate authentication flow into `services/auth.ts`. The structure will align with Firebase, but fully simulated. I'll include components for email/password login and signup. `Onboarding.tsx` will include a custom habit form with name, icon, interest, and daily schedule options. `App.tsx` will manage auth states ("loading," "unauthenticated," "authenticated") and render components accordingly. The approach ensures a seamless, simulated auth experience.


**Developing Auth Mock**

I'm now creating a mock Firebase Auth in `services/auth.ts`, mimicking its API with `signInWithEmailAndPassword`, `createUserWithEmailAndPassword`, and `onAuthStateChanged`. 1. Task Button (Main Entry Point)

A dedicated button on the home screen labeled “Tasks & Projects”.
When tapped, it opens a clean list view of:

Tasks

Projects

Deadlines

Progress status

Minimal, organized, Apple-style layout.

Inside the task section, users can:

• Add a new task

Fields:

Title

Description (optional)

Due date

Priority

Category/Tag

Add to project (optional)

Reminder toggle

• Add a new project

Fields:

Project name

Description

Start & end date

Add multiple tasks under it

Progress bar auto-updates

Every task or project with a date is automatically synced into the in-app calendar.

Calendar view includes:

Daily view

Weekly view

Monthly view

Color-coded tasks/projects

Tap any date to see all items due

When setting a task/project date, users can choose:

At time of deadline

1 hour before

1 day before

Custom reminder

Recurring reminders (for habits or repeated tasks)

Tasks due today

Upcoming tasks

Project percentages

Overdue tasks (small red indicator)

AI-generated task suggestions based on user patterns

Auto-prioritization

Sync with Google Calendar / Apple Calendar

Timer / Focus-mode inside tasks

✔ UI wireframes for this feature
